From the Tralee and Dingle Railway Company we
have received a very pretty, shall we call it illustrated,
guide book, ““ Thro’ Rare West Kerry.””  We once
spent a Christmas beyond Dingle, and our first
acquaintance with the Tralee and Dingle Railway was
a journey commenced in the dark.  The sublime
scenery of that route lives in our memory; by the time
we reached Annascaul the first grey streaks of dawn
were making the outlines of the mountains visible.
Though we have not been all over the West of Europe,
we are more than willing to beliove this railway guide
book, when it says: ¢ Scarcely any other railway in the
West of Furope opens up so superb a prospect in so
short a space of time and distance.””

From one point of view it is a good job the Horse
Show is over. Now that some daily papers have taken
to reproducing photographs, the days of the Forse Show.
were rather trying on those who read newspapers, and
as & matter of course glance at the pictures. A man
one never heard of is snapped talking to a lady one
never heard of, and this scene is dished up as a pictur
merely because both have titles. ; ;

mhere§bmg instead of the wearisome and upstarting
snapping of mere titled nobodies. After all, though a
camera is mechanieal, the man: who uses it has room
for some play of brains and sense in the selection of
the scenes or persons snapped. Ividently a title sends
the poor newspaper camera brigade off their balance.
We doubt if the best friends of some of the titled ladies
recently snapped felt that any debt-of gratitude was
due fo the snapper.

Bgt, perhaps, these newspapers know their main
public, and they may be, from a commercial point of
view, correct in ng silly scenes to silly readers.  The
public are very silly in many respects. Wo happened to
be at Loughshinny on the Thursday when the waterplane
was being dismantled. The plucky, Australian aviator,
Hawker, was there looking after things. e got a fine
hearty reception as was only fittin Like many, if
not most, men of real pluck and daring, ho appeared

He took it all right; perhaps he liked it up to a certain

remain in peace.  Autograph hunting had become
quite a virulently contagious disease, and out {o the

to talk of her boots-and stockings, and eventually she
was one of the few who werc unsuccessful. We must

When a fellow like Carson plays the fool and leads on
people with false cv false hopes, false threats, and
false promises, it is as well {o have him out and about
when the climax ves, and a deceived Orange mob
clamours to him for a lead that he is powerless to give.
Of course, a fellow like Carson would like to be in jail
at that time; it would be a safe retreat. And when he
came out he could swagger about what would have hap-
pened had he been able to conduct the campaign.

Larkin stands for the sympathetic strike, and that is
an impossible labour policy. The sympathetic strike
is somewhat like boycotting—under some circumstances
and in some degre: nd conditions there is not much,
if anything, to be said against it; but as an organised
weapon carried out by terrorism v = £ad ncthing to say
for it. In Ireland, owing to the absence of Home Rule
and owing to the natural and traditional partial dis-
respeet into which Law—presented to us so largely as
the will of an outside force working aguinst the nationa
will—has fallen, it is hard to expect, the public view
to be clear on many matters involving Law and order.
In a well-ordered state the legitimate and established
authorities ought to be supreme in secular affairs,

. workingmen and employers looking for protection. and
impartial treatment from the authorities. The organisa-
tion of all labour in a class would not fit in with the
work of a well-ordered state, for in many ways it would
tend to usurp its authority, and an Ireland under Home
Rule would see that very clearlv. The work-
ing world would be impossible under the organised and
terrorised sympathetic strike. And after all that is
what the new Labour movement aims towards. The
movement is on the side of the employee, and almost

any little dispute would involve part of the unoffending
country. Take the ha’penny dreadful trouble. Traders
should not sell it; if they did, they were blacklegs; the
railways should not carry it, else the men should %o
out, for the porter who handled the paper was a scab,
and no other porters, guards, drivers and so forth

the men should go out; people should not buy it;
compositors should not set it up, and so on. And all
the time the responsibility for trouble might be very
much in question as between employers and employees.
A Labour movement of that kind is unthinkable.

The first prominent appearance in Ireland of the
organised sympathetic strike was about two years ago
when a timber dispute precipitated the great railway
strike. ~ We expressed ourselves pretty freely then
against an organised sympathetic strike, which is in-
dustrially absurd and impossible; though we also con-
demned the Great Southern Railway for not giving con-
sidering time for those concerned, including the public,
before things were precipitated. Many of the men, in
the Great Southern Railway were smarting under the
since re-exported Hastings Dent. The Great Southern
are now rid of Dent. About two years ago we more
than once pointed out the futility and impossibility of
the weapon of the sympathetic stiiki In our issue of
September the 23rd, 1911, we remarked: ‘‘ ¢ Sym-
pathy * strikes might go on ad nfinitum. If a passen-
ger bought a trousers that was made in a ‘ sweated ’
and non-union house, and if he also bought a ticket
for Dalkey, and, dressed in the non-union trousers,
seated himself in a carriage, what is to prevent the
whole railway line from being dislocated? If the man:
in the non-union trousers, insists upon being carried,
and if the checkers, guards, porters, drivers and stokers:
refuse o work on the railway that insists on carrying
a passenger in a non-union pair of trousers, is the line
to be paralysed?’’ The timber dispute precipitated the
railway strike here, and the British railwaymen did not
2o on strike in sympathy with the railwaymen in Ire-
land. .

Ireland, we are pleased to think, will have none of it.

In 1911 we deprecated the effort of the Dublin Cham-
ber of Commerce to establish an organisation of em-
ployers. We devoted an article to the matter, and in
the course of that article we said: ‘“If ‘ sympathy’
strikes are a nuisance and a danger, how are the ‘ sym-
pathy * lock-outs to be regarded as good? If a com-
bination of what we may call variegated labour is un-
desirable, how is a combination of varviegated em-
ployers desirable? ’ TFurther on we said: ‘‘ An atti-
tude of combined labour against employers is an atbi-
tude of class against class, which would not meet with
the sympathy of the Irish people An attitude of ‘ the
employer is the enemy of labour’ has only to be dis-
cussed in Ireland in order to be brushed aside as absurd,
pernicious, uneconomic and unpatriotic.”

The organised sympathetic strile backed by threats
and violence is now being met by something like a
sympathetic lock-out policy. And here we have all
the elements for a struggle that if it lasted long enough
would do enormous economic damage to the com-
munity. The people do not want strikes nor lock-outs
nor street rows nor baton charges. If now and again
we must have a strike it ought at least be called
deliberately and on careful calculation, and its scope
should be localised as much as possible. The present
lock-out policy may make a claim that it is working in
self-defence. We hope in the near future that both
it and the organised sympathetic strile, as we know
it, will be matters of the past.

Last weelk we expressed the hope that the Tram strike
would not be availed of by outsiders for a repetition
of the disorderly scenes that Dublin witnessed not so
long ago. Unfortunately the rioting of that time has
already been exceeded in connection with the present
labour troubles, and accounts of the daily, or rather
nightly, scenes of the riot are disagreeable reading for
Dublin citizens. No doubt Liverpool and other places
in Great Britain have had their riots, but it is poor
consolation at the best for Irishmen that Inglishmen
have been worse in the rowdy line. Already some of
the anti-Home Rule papers in England are trying to
make capital out of the disturbance, but we in Ireland
who know something of what goes on duriug English
labour troubles can afford to treat that sort of thing
with contempt; besides we know that if Home Rule
were esablished, and if the public attitude towards the
police were normal, that the work of putting down
were established, and if the public attitude towards the
easier than it is at present. No doubt the enemies of
Home Rule are not altogether displeased that Dublin
at this particular time should be the scene of street
riots.

IRISH HEALTH RESORTS.
LISDOONVARNA.

THOUS NDS of Irish people when taking an annual

holiday cross over to England. As health resorts
such places as Harrogate, Buxton, Bournemouth ave
largely supported from Ireland. Medical advisers feel
safe in recommending Buxton for rheumatism, Harro-
gate for stomach troubles, Bournemouth for general
loss of tone. In this way many thousands of pounds
are annually transferred from- Ireland to England.

Up to the present our Irish health-places have not
been much studied. Places like Lisdoonvarna have
to work their way into public notice by sheer merit,
and this is a slow process.  The merits of Lisdoon-
varna seems, however, to have ensured its success.
Sixty years ago there was only one hotel, now there
are about a dozen.

In the middle of August, 1913, the visitors at Lis-
doonvarna were about four thousand (4,000), and dur-
ing the whole season, which ordinarily is from June to
October, the total number of visitors is probably 25,000
or 30,000.

From the point of view of health, the wells—sulphur,
“magnesia, iron—are a great attraction. The chief well
—the Sulphur Spa—is not only drunk, but is also used
for baths. Its advantages for rheumatism seems to be
undoubted. A group of women were near the well and
one of them was giving her experience.  She had
suffered from rheumatism, and she took baths, and
now, said she, *‘ I have neither pain nor ache, thank
” In its way, I thought this a very good
certifieate.

To my mind the air in Lisdoonvarna has quite as
much to do with the improvement of the visitors in
health as have the waters. The broad Atlantic Ocean
is only three or four miles away, Lisdoonvarna itself
stands at an clevation varying from 400 feet to 600
feet, and the breezes from the Atlantic before they
reach Lisdoonvarna pass over a large bog. IHence the
air has the advantage of absolute purity and of such
modifications as can be introduced into it by sea, bog,
and mountain.  On a fine warm day, with a slight
breeze from the west, it is a positive pleasure—an in-
tense pleasure—to breathe the air on the hill which
overlooks the * bog-road.” Mo breathe this air has
much the same effect as to dvink the very best cham-

-pagne. It exhilarates. One feels that it is a happiness
- to be alive.” Under

ch circumstances the question,
** Is life worth livi seems a silly one. On ‘‘ Con-
stitution Hill ** life is decidedly worth living; the mere
act of breathing is a keenly-felt pleasure.

On fine a great many of the visitors go on
excursions he Cliffs of Moher, Lahinch, Blackhe
and Ba aughan are the favourite drives. The Cliffs
of Mohe nificent—a tremendous sea-wall. A
modern French writer—E. A. Martel—-in a singularly
interesting book on the cliffs and caves of Treland and
England, says—(I give a translation)—

J nearly perpendicular Cliffs of Moher

. astonished me more than the Giant’s Causeway.
By their height and their perpendicularity they
hold a record, and surpass everything that T have
seen in sea-cliffs. The Cliffs of Slieve Lengue and
of the North Cape (Norway) are hig
they are not as perpendicular, and the C
Havre and of Treport are not half as high.
everything into account the Clifis of Moher are pro-
bably the finest in the world. The view from the
cliffs stretches out into the Atlantic, and embraces

the Aran Isles. He who has not seen the Moher
Cliffs does not know what a sea-wall is!

Lahinch, nine or ten miles from Lisdoonvarna, is
now well known for its goli-links, which are supposed
to be the best in the British Isles.

The drive round Blackhead to Ballyvaughan and back
to Lisdoonvarna by the celebrated Corkscrew Hill re-
minds one of the celebrated road round the north-west
sea coast of Antrim. ;

The Aran Isles are easily accessible from Lisdoon-
varna, and the Aran Isles reveal a new world—or it
might be more correct to say a very old world, for the
people in the Aran Isles have changed comparatively
little during the past fifteen hundred years. The
Cyclopean forts, and especially Dun Zingus and Dun
Conor, will more than repay the trouble of a visit.
There is little doubt that they are over two thousand

Amonf the visitors whom I met at Lisdoonvarna was a
Catholic clergyman from Bivmingham. He was English
by birth, and in accent, manner, etc., so that I ex-
pressed some surprise at his having come to Lisdoon-
varna. He said he had often heard of it—in England
.—as a health-resort, and he had seen it in railway
advertisements in Birmingham.

Talking of clergymen reminds me of the fact that on
one Sunday the Masses said in the Catholic Church
numbered ninety (90). Probably no other church in
Ireland and few churches in the world had as many
Masses.

The fact that clergymen who need a rest and change
of air frequent Lisdoonvarna in such numbers is one
of the best possible proofs of the salubrity of the place.
No persons are better able to judge of such matters, and
most of them have the advantage of speaking from their
own personal experience. They come year after year,
So, indeed, do the lay people. ‘T have been coming
now for three years —or five years, or seven years—is
an expression commonly heard. Sometimes’ it .takes
the form: It is now fifteen years—or twenty—since I
paid my first visit to Lisdoonvarna.’’ 5

The recent great meeting in London of medical doc-
tor: me 8,000 or 10,000 were present—was largely
interested in world-wide problems, but let us hope that
in the near future our Irish medical men will find time
to inguire into and make known the special advantages
of Lisdoonvarna. 3

The Gaelic League is out to break down the tyranny
of English fashions and amusements, to free opinion
from the ways and conventions of English life. We
have ‘had twenty years of feisanna and aeridheactha
organising, during which time the dancing element has
received, perhaps, more than its due share of promi-
nence in the Gaelic League programme: we have had
numerous champion hornpipe and other step dancers
dpcon_‘ated with medals, laurels and various minor dis-
tinctions. The solo step dancers have been glorified;
every Tom, Dick and Harry running a concert sticks a
hornpipe item on the programme to give the entertain-
ment an Irish flavour—to please the general public
and placate Gaelic Leaguers. This exploiting of the
hornpipe and neglect of the cultivation—or rather the
spreading a knowledge of-—of the figure dances is
responsible for the idea that many people have, viz.,
that hornpipe dancing is synonymous for all that-the
Gaelic League can show in the way of dancing. The
policy of the Gaelic League should be to provide the
greatest amount of enjoyment for the largest number
and aim at popularising the national figure dances, such
as ‘‘ The Walls of Limerick,”” ““ The Waves of Tory,”
The Sixteen-Hand, The Light-Hand Reel, ete., ete.
\We have not, it is true, a very large stock of figure
dances; but those that we have are excellent substi-
tutes for the Lancers and *‘ The Two Step.”  The
National dances are neither strenuous nor wearisome,
but I think readers of the Leaper will agree that an
all-night dance composed of Lancers and the Two
Steps—which constitute the programme of dances at
balls and social functions outside the Gaelic League—
is a very wearisome experience. The catalogue of
foreign dancers is practically the same at the Cross
Roads and the ‘‘ middle pieces ’’ throughout the coun-
try—a monotonous round of ““ Lancers ”’ and ** Quad-
rilles.”” It is doubtful if the foreign dances would be
so much in vogue were it not for the 1
‘“ catching.”” The ‘ Catch-as-Catch-can ” tac
introduced, I am informed, about twenty-five or thir
years ago into the foreign dances; and with them we
imported a good deal of the vulgarity and grotesqueness
that are now characteristic of such exhibitions of the
“light fantastic.””  Leaving out the national aspect,
if it was only for the sake of vs by the introduction
of a few Irish figure dances would be a welcome
change.

We have had twenty years of hornpiping and double
jigging, and it is time that we came out of the Cata-
combs and popularised the graceful and picturesque
figure-dances of the Gael. The craze for the foreign
figure dancing is as great to-day at the Cross-road
dances, harvest homes and other social gatherings as
it was before the Gaelic League was started, simply
because the country folk have had little or no opportu-
nity of seeing the native product. The National dances
are not—any more than the Irish language or Irish
music—the property of the Gaelic League; and in a
normal Ireland they should have pride of place in all
social functions organised by Irishmen and women—

to the faith of their fathers, to which they are subjected,

the wretched inmates be taught to renounce that form
of Christianity in which they have been brought up,
then it is time to pause when English money is sought
